_provenance.note: This Stage 1 JSON was reconstructed from the currently-published cards so external Stage 2 (Gemini bias) and Stage 3 (Codex fact-check) reviews can verify what readers see. It is not the original analysis — the original pipeline was not run on this issue.

headline: Islamic-State Debate Returns as PAS Anchors PN State Governments in Northern Malaysia
context: PAS directly governs Kelantan, Terengganu and Kedah, and anchors the PN coalition holding Perlis. Several state assemblies have tabled Shariah-jurisdiction expansion bills.

cards.text: PAS controls Kelantan, Terengganu and Kedah and anchors PN's Perlis state government. The Islamic-state debate is back on the agenda.
cards.sub: Northern state assemblies have tabled bills expanding Shariah court jurisdiction. The Federal Court continues to police the line.

cards.h: The Federal Constitution limits Shariah courts to matters in the State List, primarily personal and family law.
cards.s: Proposed expansions in Kelantan, Terengganu and Kedah would touch areas adjacent to federal criminal jurisdiction.
cards.d: The Federal Constitution limits Shariah courts to matters in the State List, primarily personal and family law. Proposed expansions in Kelantan, Terengganu and Kedah would touch areas adjacent to federal criminal jurisdiction.

cards.h: Federal Court rulings in recent years have repeatedly affirmed that civil law prevails where state Shariah enactments overreach the State List.
cards.s: Cases challenging state enactments that touch federal subjects have generally been struck down on constitutional grounds.
cards.d: Federal Court rulings in recent years have repeatedly affirmed that civil law prevails where state Shariah enactments overreach the State List. Cases challenging state enactments that touch federal subjects have generally been struck down on constitutional grounds.

cards.h: Malay-Muslim attitudes on Islamic governance are not monolithic. The gap between 'Islamic values' and 'full hudud implementation' is wide in survey data.
cards.s: Polling consistently shows broad sympathy for Islamic principles in public life and much narrower support for the strict criminal-law version.
cards.d: Malay-Muslim attitudes on Islamic governance are not monolithic. The gap between 'Islamic values' and 'full hudud implementation' is wide in survey data. Polling consistently shows broad sympathy for Islamic principles in public life and much narrower support for the strict criminal-law version.

cards.text: The debate is framed as secular-versus-Islamic. Most Malay-Muslims want neither extreme. The middle ground has no political champion.
Like asking a country to choose between two rooms in a house that has 15. The binary framing erases the space where most people actually live.
cards.sub: The constitutional middle ground is where most voters sit, and where no party currently camps.

cards.text: The constitution already accommodates Islamic law within limits. The real debate is the scope of dual jurisdiction, and who decides where the line falls.

confidence: Low — derived from published cards, original brief not on disk; submit for retroactive Stage 2/3 verification
